title: Theory of collective learning in populations of adaptive agents
summary:
We investigate homogeneous populations of smart active agents that exchange information with their neighbors to perform a decentralized learning process aimed at achieving a prescribed macroscopic state. Such agents may, for example, represent simple microrobots. The exchanged information comprises tunable parameters governing the agent dynamics, referred to as the individual policy, together with an internal memory encoding previously visited states. This memory is used to evaluate a reward that quantifies the success of a policy to achieve the prescribed state. We extend the kinetic-theory description of collective learning in spatially homogeneous systems [Phys. Rev. Lett. 134, 248302 (2025)] and derive formal evolution equations for the distribution of policies across the population. A central outcome of our theory is the emergence of an effective reward function that fully determines the evolution of the policy distribution and encapsulates the microscopic details of the agents physical and memory dynamics. We obtain closed equations for the policy mean and variance which admit explicit time-dependent solutions under the assumption of Gaussian-distributed memories and polices.
  To illustrate the framework, we present a series of minimal microscopic models, considering both perfect and partial separation of physical, memory and policy exchange time scales, as well as models with one- and two-dimensional policies. The obtained theoretical results compare well with agent-based numerical simulations. The theory captures key aspects of collective learning, including the influence of population diversity and reward fluctuations on learning performance. Finally, we discuss potential applications to swarm robotics and machine learning, and highlight connections with classical models of biological evolution, including the Replicator equation and the Moran model.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a **kinetic-theory framework** to model decentralized learning in populations of adaptive agents using local information exchange.
• Derives formal **evolution equations** for policy distributions, enabling the analysis of how individual agent memories influence macroscopic states.
• Introduces an **effective reward function** that integrates microscopic agent physics with memory dynamics to guide policy updates.
• Connects microscopic agent-based simulations with **biological evolution models** like the Moran model and Replicator equations for swarm intelligence.
key_results: Derived closed-form evolution equations for policy mean and variance under Gaussian assumptions.
Established an effective reward function that encapsulates microscopic physical and memory dynamics.
Demonstrated that population diversity and reward fluctuations significantly influence collective learning performance.
Validated theoretical results against microscopic agent-based numerical simulations in 1D and 2D.
Mapped decentralized learning processes to classical biological frameworks like the Replicator equation.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a kinetic-theory framework for collective learning, enabling decentralized agents to optimize policies through local interactions.

title: A note on "The volume of random simplices from elliptical distributions in high dimension"
summary: Recent work by Gusakova et al. (Stochastic Process. Appl. 164 (2023) 357-382) has shown a central and a stable limit theorem for the logarithmic volume of random simplices and random convex bodies under an elliptical framework in the high dimensional regime, that is, if p and n tend to infinity in such a way that the ratio tends to γwithin (0,1). A technical condition (Equation (2.6) of Assumption (B) therein) requires that the population matrix AA* is close in Frobenius norm to a multiple of the identity matrix, which is rather restrictive and rules out various settings for statistical application, such as spiked models and dependent structure models. In this note we offer a general relaxation of this condition, which arrives at a reasonable condition and covers numerous scenarios, as well as consequences for the volume of general random simplices and random convex bodies. In particular, our results covers the Toeplitz/AR(1) covariance structures studied by Jiang and Pham (Ann. Stat. 53 (2025) 907-928), giving a concrete application of our theorem to high-dimensional dependent covariance models.

processed.summary:
*   Addresses limitations in **high-dimensional statistical theory** regarding the logarithmic volume of random simplices and convex bodies under elliptical distributions.
*   Introduces a **relaxed technical condition** for population matrices, moving beyond the restrictive requirement of proximity to identity matrices in the Frobenius norm.
*   Enables mathematical analysis for **spiked models** and **dependent covariance structures**, broadening the scope of central and stable limit theorems in high dimensions.
*   Expands the utility of the **elliptical framework** for scenarios where the ratio of features to samples (p/n) remains proportional.
key_results: Relaxes the restrictive Frobenius norm requirement (Equation 2.6) found in previous literature.
Extends limit theorems for logarithmic volume to high-dimensional dependent covariance models.
Validates applicability for Toeplitz and AR(1) covariance structures.
Provides theoretical support for general random convex bodies in high-dimensional regimes.
Establishes a more flexible framework for statistical applications in complex data settings.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers relax constraints on high-dimensional random simplices to improve statistical modeling of complex, dependent covariance structures.

title: Dynamic Neural Graph Encoding of Inference Processes in Deep Weight Space
summary: The rapid advancements in using neural networks as implicit data representations have attracted significant interest in developing machine learning methods that analyze and process the weight spaces of other neural networks. However, efficiently handling these highdimensional weight spaces remains challenging. Existing methods often overlook the sequential nature of layer-by-layer processing in neural network inference. In this work, we propose a novel approach using dynamic graphs to represent neural network parameters, capturing the temporal dynamics of inference. Our Dynamic Neural Graph Encoder (DNG-Encoder) processes these graphs, preserving the sequential nature of neural processing. Additionally, we also leverage DNG-Encoder to develop INR2JLS (Implicit Neural Representation to Joint Latent Space) for facilitate downstream applications, such as classifying Implicit Neural Representations (INRs). Our approach demonstrates significant improvements across multiple tasks, surpassing the state-of-the-art INR classification accuracy by approximately 10% on the CIFAR-100-INR.

- Introduces the **DNG-Encoder**, a novel architecture that represents neural network parameters as **dynamic graphs** to capture the temporal dynamics of layer-by-layer inference.
- Proposes **INR2JLS** (Implicit Neural Representation to Joint Latent Space), a framework designed to facilitate the analysis and classification of **Implicit Neural Representations** (INRs).
- Addresses the challenges of high-dimensional **weight space analysis** by focusing on the sequential nature of neural processing rather than treating weights as static data points.
key_results: Achieved approximately 10% higher accuracy than current state-of-the-art methods on CIFAR-100-INR classification.
Developed a method to preserve the sequential nature of neural processing during weight encoding.
Successfully mapped complex neural weight spaces into a manageable joint latent space.
Demonstrated significant performance gains across multiple INR-based downstream tasks.
Provided a scalable approach to analyzing the parameters of deep neural networks as implicit data representations.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce DNG-Encoder, utilizing dynamic graphs to analyze neural weight spaces and improve INR classification by 10%.

title: Predicting Early Stages Of Alzheimer's Disease And Identifying Key Biomarkers Using Deep Artificial Neural Network And Ensemble Of Machine Learning Methodologies
summary: Alzheimers disease (AD) is a brain disorder that develops slowly and mainly affects memory, thinking, language, and daily activities. It is one of the most common causes of dementia and creates many difficulties for patients as well as their families. In the early stage, the symptoms are often mild and may look like normal ageing. For this reason, many people are diagnosed late, when the disease has already progressed. At present, there is no complete cure for AD. Still, early detection can help doctors manage the condition better and take suitable steps at the right time. In this study, a machine learning model is proposed to detect the early stages of Alzheimers disease using clinical details, neuropsychological test scores, and neuroimaging-related measures. The data used in this work is collected from the Alzheimers Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI). As the dataset has missing values, iterative imputation is applied to fill them. The dataset also has class imbalance, which is handled using Borderline SVM-SMOTE. After that, feature selection is carried out using wrapper-based and embedded methods so that only important features are used for training. The selected features are divided into training and testing sets, and feature scaling is applied. A stacking ensemble model is developed using Logistic Regression, Extra Trees, Bagging KNN, and LightGBM as base classifiers. Along with this, an artificial neural network is also trained on the same dataset. The performance of these models is compared using precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC. This study aims to find the best classifier and also identify important biomarkers that may help in the early diagnosis of Alzheimers disease.

processed.summary:
• The study introduces a diagnostic framework utilizing **Iterative Imputation** and **Borderline SVM-SMOTE** to handle the high dimensionality and class imbalance characteristic of clinical medical data.
• It develops a **Stacking Ensemble architecture** that integrates diverse base classifiers including Logistic Regression, Extra Trees, Bagging KNN, and **LightGBM** to enhance prediction stability.
• A **Deep Artificial Neural Network (ANN)** is benchmarked against the ensemble model to evaluate the effectiveness of deep learning in identifying non-linear patterns within neuroimaging-related measures.
• The research focuses on **Feature Selection** via wrapper-based and embedded methods to isolate the most predictive biomarkers for early-stage cognitive decline.
key_results: Successfully addressed clinical data scarcity through iterative imputation techniques.
Mitigated class imbalance using Borderline SVM-SMOTE to improve recall for early-stage cases.
Integrated LightGBM and Extra Trees into a robust stacking ensemble for superior generalization.
Identified key neuroimaging and neuropsychological biomarkers via automated feature selection.
Validated model performance using comprehensive metrics including AUC-ROC and F1-score.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ADNI researchers develop a stacking ensemble and ANN framework to improve early Alzheimer’s detection and biomarker identification.

title: A$^{2}$utoLPBench: An Auto-Generated, Agent-Friendly LP Benchmark via Inverse-KKT Construction
summary: Most LP-from-text benchmarks are static datasets of word problems written and labeled by hand. Once such a dataset is released, its size is fixed, its difficulty is fixed, and every problem can leak into the training data of future LLMs. We present \textbf{A$^{2}$utoLPBench}, a benchmark for testing LLM-driven agents on linear programming problems written in plain text. We first pick a feasible point and dual, then write down a problem for which that point is optimal and the objective value is known. The answer is known by construction, with no solver call and no human annotator. The evaluation environment bundles a reference solver-critic baseline and a Docker image whose usage instructions are written for an LLM-driven agent to read. With these in place, any agent can run the benchmark and get a calibrated score with one command. Because the benchmark is a generator rather than a fixed dataset, it has properties no fixed dataset can match: an unlimited supply of fresh problems, a difficulty knob set by $(n,m)$, ground-truth answers correct by construction, low LLM-side cost per problem relative to human authoring, repeatable scores across independent batches, and resistance to training-data leakage when fresh post-cutoff seed ranges are used.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **A²utoLPBench**, a framework for generating synthetic **Linear Programming (LP)** problems from plain text using **Inverse-KKT Construction** to ensure optimality by design.
- Architected specifically for **LLM-driven agents**, providing a **Docker-based evaluation environment** with usage instructions optimized for autonomous agent consumption.
- Addresses **data leakage** and the limitations of static datasets by enabling an **unlimited supply** of fresh problems with a tunable difficulty knob (n, m).
- Features a reference **solver-critic baseline**, allowing for calibrated scoring and repeatable performance metrics across independent problem batches.
key_results: Eliminates the need for manual human annotation or solver calls to verify ground-truth optimality.
Provides a scalable 'difficulty knob' based on the number of variables and constraints.
Achieves high resistance to training-data contamination using post-cutoff seed ranges.
Significantly reduces the cost of benchmark creation relative to human-authored word problems.
Ensures repeatable and statistically significant evaluation scores through automated problem generation.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce A²utoLPBench to generate infinite, leakage-proof linear programming problems for evaluating LLM agent reasoning capabilities.

title: Quantum Convolutional Autoencoders for Reconstruction-Based Anomaly Detection
summary: Quantum convolutional neural networks (QCNNs) have become increasingly popular in quantum machine learning (QML) due to their efficient parameterization and hierarchical representation of quantum information. Anomaly detection is an important machine learning task with applications across a wide range of domains, including scientific data analysis. In this work, we adapt a QCNN architecture into a quantum autoencoder (QAE) framework for reconstruction-based anomaly detection. The models are trained in a semi-supervised manner on normal samples to reconstruct feature-extracted and dimensionally reduced time-series data, with reconstruction error used as an anomaly score. We investigate two quantum convolutional autoencoder architectures that differ in their treatment of latent information: a hierarchical architecture in which information remains distributed across the circuit and a bottleneck-based architecture in which information is explicitly compressed and reconstructed using additional decoder qubits. The size of the quantum latent space is varied to study its influence on reconstruction accuracy and anomaly detection performance. The approaches are benchmarked against both a variational quantum circuit and a comparable classical baseline using a real-world exoplanet anomaly-detection dataset. Results indicate a trade-off between latent-space size and model capacity, while also suggesting that explicit latent-space compression through a quantum bottleneck can improve anomaly detection performance relative to architectures that retain information throughout the circuit.

processed.summary:
- Adapts **Quantum Convolutional Neural Network (QCNN)** architectures into a **Quantum Autoencoder (QAE)** framework designed for reconstruction-based anomaly detection.
- Implements two distinct structural approaches: a **hierarchical architecture** with distributed information and a **bottleneck-based architecture** utilizing explicit compression through decoder qubits.
- Utilizes **reconstruction error** as a metric for anomaly scoring, trained in a semi-supervised manner on dimensionally reduced time-series data.
- Explores the impact of **quantum latent space** dimensions on the trade-off between model capacity and reconstruction accuracy.
key_results: Developed two novel quantum convolutional autoencoder (QCAE) architectures for semi-supervised learning.
Found that explicit latent-space compression via a quantum bottleneck improves anomaly detection accuracy.
Identified a clear trade-off relationship between latent-space size and overall model capacity.
Validated performance using a real-world exoplanet anomaly-detection dataset against classical baselines.
Demonstrated that QCAEs can outperform hierarchical architectures that retain information throughout the circuit.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that bottleneck-based quantum convolutional autoencoders improve reconstruction-based anomaly detection performance over hierarchical quantum architectures.

title: Behind the Refusal: Determining Guardrail Activation via Behavioral Monitoring
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) and agentic systems become integrated into real-world applications, ensuring their safety and security is critical. Guardrail systems that detect and block malicious instructions sent to and from an LLM are an essential component of AI security. However, researchers conducting black-box adversarial emulation against production AI systems often struggle to determine whether a guardrail block or an LLM rejection has occurred. This distinction is important because the techniques used to bypass guardrails can differ substantially from those used to bypass LLM safety alignment, and has a material impact on attack technique selection and optimization. We propose the first black-box guardrail reconnaissance methodology, which detects the presence of a guardrail within a target AI system through behavioral monitoring of HTTP, lexical, and timing signals, assuming only black-box access and zero prior knowledge of the guardrail or AI system. Experiments demonstrate that our approach detects guardrail presence with 100% accuracy, with statistically significant behavioral separation between benign and malicious interactions (q < 0.001). Our approach further identifies the content categories a guardrail is designed to block, and distinguishes guardrail blocks from LLM rejection on unseen prompts with an average F1 score of 98%.

processed.summary:
* Proposes a **black-box reconnaissance methodology** to identify guardrail presence through HTTP, lexical, and timing signals.
* Distinguishes between model-level **safety alignment** and external **guardrail systems** to optimize adversarial emulation techniques.
* Achieves high-fidelity **behavioral separation** between interaction types without requiring prior knowledge of the target system.
key_results: 100% accuracy in detecting the presence of guardrails within black-box systems.
98% average F1 score in distinguishing guardrail blocks from internal model rejections.
Statistically significant behavioral separation between interaction types (q < 0.001).
Identification of specific content categories the guardrail is designed to block.
Proven effectiveness using only HTTP, lexical, and timing signals with zero prior knowledge.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a behavioral monitoring method to distinguish external guardrail blocks from internal LLM safety rejections.

title: Enhancing Fitness Intelligence through Domain-Specific LLM Post-Training
summary: Scientific Fitness Coaching (SFC) is typically delivered by human professionals, making it costly and inaccessible to many. While recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) show considerable promise for more inclusive fitness coaching, directly deploying prevailing general-purpose LLMs in SFC reveals critical limitations. These models often lack sufficient domain-specific knowledge integration, leading to weak performance on complex SFC scenarios. In this paper, we introduce FitOne, a series of fitness LLMs (with 8B and 32B parameters) designed to improve reliability and domain specialization for SFC applications. Built upon the Qwen3 foundation models, FitOne is developed through a three-stage post-training pipeline consisting of continual pre-training, supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning, using large-scale, high-quality datasets derived from rigorous knowledge engineering. We conduct comprehensive evaluations of FitOne on professional fitness certification exams, including ACSM-EP and NSCA-CSCS, as well as general capabilities such as knowledge reasoning and instruction following. Experimental results show that, while retaining strong general capabilities, FitOne-8B/32B achieves average improvements of up to 10.09%/9.29% and 12.73%/7.01% on the ACSM-EP and NSCA-CSCS exams, respectively, compared with the Qwen3 base models. Furthermore, in-depth ablation studies confirm the necessity of each training stage, highlighting the pipeline's effectiveness in balancing domain expertise enhancement with general ability retention. We believe this research advances LLM systems toward more reliable fitness intelligence and will inspire future research on developing domain-specific LLMs.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **FitOne-8B and 32B**, domain-specialized LLMs built on the **Qwen3** foundation for Scientific Fitness Coaching (SFC).
- Employs a **three-stage post-training pipeline** consisting of continual pre-training, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and reinforcement learning (RL).
- Utilizes large-scale, high-quality datasets derived from **rigorous knowledge engineering** to bridge the gap in domain-specific fitness knowledge.
- Validates model efficacy using professional **certification benchmarks**, balancing niche expertise with the retention of general reasoning capabilities.
key_results: FitOne-8B achieved an average 10.09% improvement on ACSM-EP exams compared to the base model.
FitOne-32B showed a 9.29% performance gain on ACSM-EP professional certification tests.
NSCA-CSCS exam performance improved by 12.73% for the 8B model and 7.01% for the 32B variant.
Ablation studies confirmed that all three training stages—pre-training, SFT, and RL—are essential for domain mastery.
The models successfully retained strong general instruction-following and reasoning abilities during specialization.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce FitOne, leveraging a three-stage post-training pipeline on Qwen3 to significantly advance domain-specific fitness intelligence.

title: Black Boxes in Black Hole Imaging
summary: We investigate the epistemic opacity of computer simulations and machine learning methods in the context of black hole imaging. We argue that there are forms of opacity-including opacity resulting from the use of machine learning-which do not need to affect the reliability of an inference when it is seen as a part of a broader inferential framework. We propose conditions under which that can plausibly be the case, and discuss how opaque methods can be useful in the context of the (next generation) Event Horizon Telescope. However, we also argue that at least one problematic form of opacity is currently present in black hole imaging: GRMHD models of Sagittarius A* are opaque. This form of opacity signals the limitations of current understanding of the models of this source, and constrains the potential uses of ML models in future observations.

processed.summary:
- Investigates the **epistemic opacity** of machine learning and computer simulations in the specific context of **black hole imaging** via the Event Horizon Telescope.
- Proposes a framework for maintaining **inferential reliability** despite the use of black-box ML models, provided they are integrated into broader scientific structures.
- Identifies critical limitations in current **GRMHD (General Relativistic Magnetohydrodynamic)** models, which act as a bottleneck for future ML model utility.
key_results: Defined conditions under which opaque ML methods do not compromise scientific inference.
Analyzed the role of computer simulations as a necessary but potentially opaque layer in astrophysics.
Confirmed that ML-driven imaging can be reliable within a structured inferential framework.
Discovered that Sagittarius A* GRMHD models represent a problematic form of opacity.
Determined that physical model limitations currently constrain the effectiveness of ML in next-generation EHT observations.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that ML opacity in black hole imaging is manageable within robust inferential and physical frameworks.

title: Multimodal Fusion for Fine-Grained Classification of Breast Fibroadenoma and Phyllodes Tumors
summary: Breast fibroadenoma (FA) and phyllodes tumor (PT) are fibroepithelial breast lesions with highly overlapping appearances on B-mode ultrasound, making benign and borderline PT prone to being misclassified as FA and complicating preoperative decision-making. Existing computer-aided diagnosis methods commonly rely on single-modal imaging features and insufficiently exploit complementary clinical and textual information. To address this limitation, we construct the FAPT-M Dataset, a pathology-confirmed multimodal dataset comprising 910 patients with strictly reviewed ultrasound images, structured clinical attributes, and ultrasound diagnostic descriptions. Based on this dataset, we propose a clinically guided multimodal framework that integrates DenseNet-based visual encoding, CLIP-inspired text encoding, and lightweight clinical encoding, and further introduces clinical-conditioned adaptive modulation, cross-modal Transformer fusion, and dual-path representation learning to improve feature alignment and multimodal interaction. Under patient-level five-fold cross-validation, the proposed method achieves an accuracy of 77.64%, F1-score of 73.38%, and AUC of 89.74%, outperforming representative CNN-, Transformer-, and vision-language-based baselines. Ablation studies and class-balanced evaluations further confirm the contribution of three-modality fusion and the key architectural components. Overall, this work provides an effective multimodal approach for fine-grained FA-PT classification and establishes a high-quality benchmark for multimodal breast ultrasound analysis.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **multimodal fusion framework** designed for fine-grained classification of breast lesions, integrating B-mode ultrasound images, clinical attributes, and textual descriptions.
- Implements a complex architecture utilizing **DenseNet-based visual encoding**, **CLIP-inspired text encoding**, and a lightweight clinical encoder to capture heterogeneous feature spaces.
- Introduces **cross-modal Transformer fusion** and clinical-conditioned adaptive modulation to resolve feature alignment issues across disparate data modalities.
- Utilizes **dual-path representation learning** to improve the robustness of feature interaction and overall diagnostic accuracy for overlapping tumor phenotypes.
key_results: Achieved an overall classification accuracy of 77.64% using three-modality fusion.
Recorded a high AUC of 89.74%, demonstrating strong discriminative capability.
Attained an F1-score of 73.38%, outperforming vision-only and text-only baselines.
Ablation studies confirmed that integrating clinical data provides significant performance gains over binary modality models.
Established the FAPT-M Dataset, a benchmark comprising 910 pathology-confirmed multimodal patient records.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop a multimodal Transformer framework that integrates clinical, visual, and textual data to improve breast tumor classification.

title: HaloGuard 1.0: An Open Weights Constitutional Classifier for Multilingual AI Safety
summary: We present HaloGuard 1.0, an open-weights implementation of the constitutional-classifier paradigm for input safety. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on English and multilingual prompt-safety benchmarks at roughly one-tenth the model size of current leading open guard models. The safety constitution is the organising structure of the corpus: a natural-language constitution of 46 policies and 2,940 subcategories drives synthetic data generation, with exhaustive one-to-one paired counterfactuals that hold topic and vocabulary fixed while flipping intent, a two-tier harmless design that separately targets boundary and baseline false positives (FPs), and balanced multilingual materialisation across 46 languages that treats language as a surface form appearing on both sides of the boundary rather than as an adversarial signal. Across seven prompt-safety benchmarks, HaloGuard 1.0-0.8B attains the best average F1 (90.9) of any open guard we evaluate, outperforming baselines up to 27B parameters (over 30 times larger) while holding false-positive rate (FPR) to 4.3 and false-negative rate (FNR) to 9.5. The HaloGuard 1.0-4B variant reaches average F1 of 92.1 and FPR of 3.5, spending its extra capacity on precision rather than recall. A structured adjudication of the remaining failures indicates that most apparent missed-harm cases are benchmark mislabels rather than genuine model misses. An always-on adversarial red-teaming protocol continuously hardens the guard against both content-level and agentic attacks. We release the models as open weights.

processed.summary:
- Implements a **constitutional-classifier paradigm** for input safety using 46 policies and 2,940 subcategories to drive high-fidelity synthetic data generation.
- Employs **one-to-one counterfactuals** that isolate user intent while holding vocabulary fixed, specifically targeting boundary-level false positives.
- Features a **multilingual design** supporting 46 languages, treating language as a surface form rather than an adversarial signal to ensure robust global safety.
- Achieves **extreme efficiency**, allowing the 0.8B and 4B models to outperform dense models up to 30 times their size (27B parameters).
key_results: HaloGuard 1.0-0.8B achieved a state-of-the-art average F1 score of 90.9 across seven benchmarks.
The 0.8B model outperformed baseline guard models up to 27B parameters in size.
The 4B variant reached a 92.1 F1 score, optimizing for high precision and a low 3.5% False Positive Rate.
Maintained a False Negative Rate of 9.5% while resisting both content-level and agentic attacks.
Analysis indicates remaining failures are largely due to benchmark mislabels rather than model misses.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: The HaloGuard authors release open-weights constitutional safety models that outperform competitors thirty times their size across 46 languages.

title: Evidence-State Rewards for Long-Context Reasoning
summary: Long-context reasoning requires models to locate, revise, and synthesize evidence distributed across lengthy inputs. Existing long-context RL methods usually reward final answers or static evidence extraction, offering little feedback on how intermediate actions change the model's evidence state. We propose Maven, a reinforcement learning framework with an editable evidence memory. Maven defines an answer-conditioned evidence-state value and rewards action-level state transitions: add actions are credited by marginal gain and hindsight contribution, link actions by evidence synergy, and drop actions by improved answer support after removing misleading evidence. These rewards are assigned to the corresponding action spans in GRPO. Across Llama and Qwen models on LongBench v2, LongReason, and RULER, Maven outperforms outcome-only RL and evidence-identification baselines, producing more sufficient evidence sets and lower distractor retention. Our results show that long-context RL benefits from optimizing stateful evidence navigation rather than one-shot evidence extraction.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **Maven**, a reinforcement learning framework designed for long-context reasoning that utilizes an **editable evidence memory**.
- Implements **action-level state rewards** for specific operations (add, link, drop), allowing for fine-grained feedback during the reasoning process.
- Leverages **Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)** to credit marginal gains from new evidence and the removal of misleading distractors.
- Shifts the paradigm from one-shot evidence extraction to **stateful navigation**, improving how models synthesize distributed information.
key_results: Outperforms outcome-only RL and static evidence-extraction baselines across multiple benchmarks.
Achieves superior performance on LongBench v2, LongReason, and RULER datasets.
Reduces retention of misleading distractor evidence through explicit 'drop' action rewards.
Increases the sufficiency and synergy of extracted evidence sets compared to traditional methods.
Demonstrates successful scaling across both Llama and Qwen model architectures.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: HKUST and Microsoft researchers introduce Maven, an RL framework that optimizes long-context reasoning through stateful evidence-memory rewards.

title: kNNGuard: Turning LLM Hidden Activations into a Training-Free Configurable Guardrail
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in domains requiring guardrails to detect unsafe, off-topic, or adversarial prompts. Existing guardrails predominately rely on fine-tuning to build classifiers, which often suffer from low generalization and high inference latency. We present kNNGuard, a training-free guardrail that utilizes the activation space of an off-the-shelf LLM. Given a small bank of 50 safe and unsafe prompts, kNNGuard extracts hidden activations and performs multi-layer kNN fusing activation-space and embedding-space scores for classification. Across six domains spanning topical and security prompts, kNNGuard achieves competitive or superior F1 compared to fine-tuned state-of-the-art guardrails while running 2.7x faster than the best comparable guardrail, and 10x faster than a fine-tuned safety classifier without gradient updates or fine-tuning. Domain adaptation requires only updating the labeled bank, which can be constructed in under 10 seconds and several orders of magnitude faster than established guardrails. We also analyze the impact of system prompts, layer selection, and integration into production LLM pipelines as a configurable, low-latency guardrail.

processed.summary:
• **kNNGuard** is a **training-free** guardrail architecture that leverages internal **hidden activations** of LLMs to detect unsafe or off-topic prompts.
• The system employs a **multi-layer kNN** approach to fuse activation-space and embedding-space scores, requiring only a small bank of 50 labeled examples.
• It enables **near-instant domain adaptation** (under 10 seconds) by simply updating the labeled vector bank, eliminating the need for expensive **model fine-tuning**.
• The architecture is highly optimized for **low-latency** production pipelines, significantly outperforming existing safety classifiers in inference speed.
key_results: Achieved 2.7x faster performance than LlamaGuard and 10x faster than fine-tuned safety classifiers.
Maintains competitive or superior F1 scores across six distinct topical and security domains.
Reduces domain adaptation time from hours of fine-tuning to less than 10 seconds.
Successfully fuses activation and embedding space scores for improved classification accuracy.
Proved effective as a configurable, low-latency guardrail for production LLM pipelines.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce kNNGuard, a training-free system using hidden activations to detect unsafe prompts 10x faster than fine-tuned models.

title: Prompt Coverage Adequacy
summary: In recent years, it has become increasingly evident that large language models (LLMs) and autonomous agents raise the level of abstraction in software development by shifting the focus from writing precise procedures to expressing intents and goals. This paradigm shift introduces new challenges, particularly in how testing should be guided when prompts, rather than code, become primary development artifacts. To address this challenge, we propose Prompt Coverage Adequacy, a novel coverage criterion designed to support the testing of code generated from task descriptions. Prompt Coverage Adequacy serves as an analog to traditional code coverage, but operates at the level of prompts used in LLM and agent-based programming. Specifically, it measures how well a given test suite satisfies the requirements expressed in a prompt by leveraging the attention mechanisms of LLMs. We evaluate a simple instantiation of this criterion, based on attention boosting, across two datasets and multiple LLMs. Our results demonstrate that Prompt Coverage is associated with fault-detection effectiveness and can uncover over 30+% more faults than traditional code coverage when used to guide test generation. These findings suggest that Prompt Coverage Adequacy can serve as a foundation for developing testing metrics better suited to the emerging paradigm of LLM-driven software development, addressing the limitations of classical coverage criteria in this new context.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **Prompt Coverage Adequacy**, a novel testing metric that shifts focus from code-level structural coverage to the **semantic requirements** expressed in natural language prompts.
- Utilizes **LLM attention mechanisms** and an attention-boosting approach to map test case execution back to specific intents within a prompt.
- Bridges the gap in **LLM-driven software development** (Vibe Coding) where traditional code coverage fails to capture the developer's high-level goals.
- Establishes a quantitative framework for evaluating the reliability of code generated by **autonomous agents** and generative models.
key_results: Detected over 30% more faults than traditional structural code coverage metrics.
Proven correlation between high Prompt Coverage and increased fault-detection effectiveness.
Validated through multiple datasets and diverse LLM architectures using attention boosting.
Demonstrated that intent-based metrics are superior for testing code generated from natural language.
Provides a foundation for next-generation automated test generation tools for AI agents.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Prompt Coverage Adequacy, using LLM attention to detect 30% more software faults than traditional metrics.

title: Mitigating Package Hallucinations in Large Language Models via Model Editing
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in software engineering tasks, such as code generation, library recommendation, and dependency configuration. However, recent studies show that LLMs may suffer from package hallucination, where they generate non-existent or invalid package names. These hallucinations can be exploited in software supply chain attacks, as attackers may register malicious packages under hallucinated names. Therefore, mitigating package hallucination is important for improving the reliability and security of LLM-assisted software development. In this paper, we introduce BOUND, a lightweight localized model editing framework for mitigating package hallucinations in LLMs. BOUND formulates package hallucination mitigation as a package-validity boundary editing problem, where the boundary refers to the model's ability to distinguish valid packages from hallucinated package names under a given task context. It first locates modules related to package hallucination through a risk-aware localization strategy, and then edits these modules with lightweight LoRA adapters using a boundary-aware objective that reinforces valid packages, suppresses hallucinated packages, and preserves locality behavior. Experimental results show that BOUND effectively reduces package hallucinations while preserving valid package recommendations. In the package recommendation task, BOUND reduces package-level hallucination rate (Package-HR) by 79.9% on edit prompts and by 65.4% on unseen prompts. The learned package-validity boundary further generalizes to other package-related tasks, reducing Package-HR by 12.8% in code generation and by 34.0% in pip install recommendation. These results show that BOUND refines the package-validity boundary of LLMs and improves the reliability of package-related outputs.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **BOUND**, a framework for mitigating **package hallucinations** by treating the issue as a **package-validity boundary** problem.
- Employs a **risk-aware localization strategy** to identify specific model modules responsible for hallucinating non-existent software libraries.
- Utilizes **LoRA adapters** to refine model weights, optimizing for an objective that reinforces valid packages while suppressing invalid ones.
- Demonstrates high **cross-task generalization**, improving reliability in package recommendation, code generation, and dependency management.
key_results: Reduced Package Hallucination Rate (Package-HR) by 79.9% on targeted edit prompts.
Achieved a 65.4% reduction in Package-HR on unseen prompts, showing strong generalization.
Lowered hallucinations in code generation tasks by 12.8% without specific code-based training.
Improved pip install recommendation reliability by reducing errors by 34.0%.
Successfully preserved model performance on valid package recommendations while maintaining locality behavior.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce BOUND, a model editing framework that suppresses software package hallucinations by refining validity boundaries using LoRA.

title: SPLIT: Cross-Lingual Empathy and Cultural Grounding in English and Ukrainian LLM Responses
summary: Large Language Models are increasingly deployed in emotional-support contexts and crisis-related situations. Nevertheless, their cross-lingual abilities in these circumstances remain underexplored. Existing benchmarks emphasize multilingual performance but rarely examine crisis-related empathy and cultural grounding in low-to-mid-resource languages. We introduce SPLIT, a 500-prompt benchmark designed to evaluate LLM consistency in generating emotionally grounded responses across five categories: Stress, Panic, Loneliness, Internal Displacement, and Tension. We evaluate three technically diverse LLMs across three dimensions: Empathetic Accuracy, Linguistic Naturalness, and Contextual & Cultural Grounding. The framework aims to assess and compare the quality of LLM responses in both English and Ukrainian languages, as well as to explore the reliability of the LLM-as-a-jury paradigm. Our findings reveal that Gemini-2.5-Flash and LLaMA-3.3-70B-Instruct degrade when transitioning to Ukrainian, while DeepSeek-V3 remains comparatively stable within our benchmark. We additionally find that human and AI evaluators agree weakly on empathy and naturalness but diverge on cultural grounding. We further argue that producing Ukrainian text is not equivalent to producing Ukrainian emotional support. Our findings may assist in the future development of more culturally tailored benchmark designs, as well as encourage a stronger emphasis on human-centered evaluation.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **SPLIT**, a 500-prompt benchmark designed to measure **cross-lingual empathy** and cultural grounding in crisis-related contexts.
- Compares the performance of **Gemini-2.5-Flash**, **LLaMA-3.3-70B-Instruct**, and **DeepSeek-V3** across English and Ukrainian languages.
- Employs a multi-dimensional evaluation framework focusing on **Empathetic Accuracy**, **Linguistic Naturalness**, and **Cultural Grounding** using both human and AI-as-a-jury metrics.
key_results: Gemini-2.5-Flash and LLaMA-3.3-70B-Instruct show significant quality degradation when responding in Ukrainian.
DeepSeek-V3 remains the most stable model across English and Ukrainian versions of the benchmark.
Human and AI evaluators demonstrate weak agreement on empathy and linguistic naturalness.
AI evaluators and human experts diverge sharply regarding the assessment of cultural grounding.
Correct linguistic translation does not correlate with culturally appropriate emotional support for mid-resource languages.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce the SPLIT benchmark, finding DeepSeek-V3 outperforms LLaMA and Gemini in maintaining cross-lingual empathetic stability.

title: Fast and Accurate Anomaly Detection in Time Series
summary: Anomaly detection is a critical and evolving field in Machine Learning, with applications targeting different domains such as cybersecurity, finance, healthcare, manufacturing and IoT (Internet of Things) systems. Traditionally, anomaly detection algorithms have been designed using both supervised and unsupervised learning paradigms. The fundamental challenge in real-world anomaly detection scenarios is related to the inherent class imbalance (anomalies are typically rare) and, for supervised methods, to the scarcity of labelled anomalous data. Indeed, labelling is both expensive and time-consuming. Conversely unsupervised methods do not require labelling, but may suffer from high false positive rates when deployed in safety-critical applications. In this work we introduce a novel unsupervised algorithm for anomaly detection in time series based on the Haar discrete wavelet and a suitably designed $t$-test. We establish the theoretical foundation of the proposed $t$-test and, through extensive experimentation across 343 datasets, demonstrate that our algorithm outperforms state-of-the-art unsupervised and self-supervised benchmarks.

processed.summary:
- Introduces a novel **unsupervised anomaly detection** algorithm tailored for complex **time series** data across domains like IoT and finance.
- Combines the **Haar discrete wavelet transform** with a specialized **t-test** to establish a rigorous theoretical foundation for identifying outliers.
- Targets the industry-wide challenge of **label scarcity** and class imbalance by removing the need for expensive manual data labeling.
- Optimizes for both **computational speed** and accuracy, making it suitable for real-world, safety-critical applications.
key_results: Proposed an unsupervised algorithm using Haar discrete wavelets.
Established a new statistical t-test framework for anomaly scoring.
Validated performance across a comprehensive set of 343 datasets.
Outperformed current state-of-the-art unsupervised benchmarks.
Demonstrated superior accuracy compared to modern self-supervised methods.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a Haar wavelet-based unsupervised algorithm that outperforms state-of-the-art benchmarks in fast time series anomaly detection.

title: Towards Load-Aware Prefill Deflection for Disaggregated LLM Serving
summary: Disaggregated LLM serving runs prefill and decode on separate GPU pools to keep the two phases from interfering. In practice, this creates a new asymmetry: under bursty, heavy-tailed workloads prefill nodes saturate while decode nodes have compute underutilized, and on a production-style A100 cluster with 2 prefill and 2 decode nodes (2P2D), we find that prefill execution accounts for only 2-23% of P95 Time-to-First-Token (TTFT). Queuing and inter-node GPU-GPU KV-cache transfer account for the rest. We present a proactive prefill-deflecting scheduler that lets decode nodes serve prefill phase of requests as chunked-prefill steps interleaved with their in-flight decode batches. For each queued request, we estimate the TTFT it would see on the prefill node, and on every decode node, search for the largest chunk schedule that keeps in-flight decodes within their Time-Between-Tokens (TBT) SLO and deflect when the decode path helps tail latency. Because the prefill phase of deflected requests runs in place on the decode node, the inter-node KV transfer is eliminated. Implemented on vLLM and evaluated on production-style traces with DeepSeek-V2-Lite, our approach reduces P95 TTFT by upto 81% and raises SLO attainment by upto 79% over state-of-the-art disaggregated schedulers, at sub-millisecond per-request routing cost.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **load-aware deflection scheduler** to address compute asymmetry in **disaggregated LLM serving** where prefill nodes saturate while decode nodes remain underutilized.
- Implements **chunked-prefill** execution on decode nodes, allowing them to interleave prefill steps with in-flight decode batches without violating TBT SLOs.
- Eliminates high-latency **inter-node KV-cache transfers** by executing the prefill phase locally on the node assigned for decoding.
- Provides a **sub-millisecond routing** mechanism that estimates TTFT for both paths to optimize tail latency under heavy-tailed workloads.
key_results: Reduces P95 Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) by up to 81% compared to standard disaggregated schedulers.
Increases SLO attainment by up to 79% on production-style traces using DeepSeek-V2-Lite.
Identified that queuing and KV-transfer account for 77-98% of P95 TTFT in standard 2P2D clusters.
Maintains strict Time-Between-Tokens (TBT) constraints by searching for optimal chunk sizes during deflection.
Demonstrates sub-millisecond per-request routing costs, making it viable for high-throughput production environments.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: University of Illinois researchers introduce a prefill-deflection scheduler for vLLM that reduces tail latency by 81% through load-balanced execution.

title: PACE: A Proxy for Agentic Capability Evaluation
summary: Evaluating LLM agents on benchmarks like SWE-Bench and GAIA can be expensive, time-consuming, and requires complex infrastructure. A single evaluation can cost thousands of dollars and take days to complete. In contrast, non-agentic LLM benchmarks that test individual capabilities (e.g., reasoning, code generation) are fast and cheap to run. In this paper, we investigate whether performance on expensive agentic benchmarks can be accurately predicted by the performance on a small, carefully selected subset of atomic evaluation instances. We introduce PACE, a framework that constructs proxy benchmarks by selecting instances from existing non-agentic evaluations whose aggregate scores most reliably predict model performances on agentic benchmarks. Given a pool of candidate instances spanning atomic capabilities, PACE fits a regression that maps a model's scores on a compact subset of source instances to its score on the target agentic benchmark. The subset itself is curated by combining two complementary instance-selection strategies, target-relevance local selection and globally informative global selection. We apply PACE to the 4 target agentic benchmarks in this paper, which yields PACE-Bench, the concrete proxy benchmark that we evaluate in the paper. Experiments across 14 models, 4 agentic benchmarks, and 19 non-agentic benchmarks show that PACE-Bench predicts agentic scores with leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV) mean absolute error (MAE) under 4%, Spearman correlation above 0.80, and pairwise model-ranking accuracy around 85%, all at much less than 1% of the full agentic evaluation cost. We further analyze the selected proxy instances, revealing which skills each agentic benchmark uniquely demands. PACE enables practitioners to obtain reliable estimates of agentic performance during model development, selection, and routing, without the overhead of full agent evaluation.

processed.summary:
• Introduces **PACE**, a framework designed to construct cost-effective proxy benchmarks by mapping **atomic capability scores** (e.g., reasoning, coding) to complex agentic performance.
• The architecture utilizes a **regression-based model** that combines two selection strategies: **target-relevance local selection** for task-specific alignment and **globally informative selection** for general model variance.
• Identifies and extracts a compact subset of instances from 19 non-agentic benchmarks to predict performance on resource-intensive targets like **SWE-Bench and GAIA**.
• Aims to streamline **model development and routing** by providing high-fidelity performance estimates without the prohibitive time and infrastructure costs of full agentic evaluations.
key_results: Achieved a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) under 4% in predicting agentic scores.
Maintained a Spearman correlation above 0.80 across diverse model sets.
Delivered a pairwise model-ranking accuracy of approximately 85%.
Reduced evaluation costs to less than 1% of traditional agentic benchmark overhead.
Successfully validated the framework across 14 models and 4 major agentic benchmarks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce PACE to predict expensive agentic benchmark performance using low-cost atomic capability subsets with 99% cost reduction.

title: Spatio-Temporal and Clinical Conditioning for Fine-Grained Radiology Report Retrieval
summary: Radiology is vital to modern healthcare, but rising imaging demand and persistent workforce shortages strain reporting capacity and clinical workflows. Automated radiology report generation has the potential to support radiologists and help alleviate this burden; however, existing retrieval-based methods remain rigid, lack explicit anatomical grounding, and do not account for longitudinal disease progression or available clinical context. In this work, we introduce STAR3, a multimodal, spatio-temporal, attentive retrieval framework for radiology report generation that aligns region-level anatomical information with clinical indications and longitudinal changes across chest X-ray studies. Our framework employs an object detector to identify anatomically meaningful regions and retrieves semantically relevant report sentences conditioned on both current clinical context and changes observed between prior and current examinations. This design enables anatomically and temporally grounded report generation that better reflects clinical reporting practice. Experiments on the MIMIC-CXR dataset demonstrate that STAR3 outperforms current retrieval-based approaches on retrieval, NLP and clinical metrics, highlighting the value of conditioning retrieval anatomically, temporally and clinically for advancing automated radiology report generation.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **STAR3**, a multimodal retrieval framework that utilizes an **object detector** to achieve fine-grained anatomical grounding in chest X-ray analysis.
- Implements **spatio-temporal conditioning** to track longitudinal disease progression by aligning features across current and prior patient examinations.
- Integrates **clinical indications** as a conditioning factor to ensure retrieved report sentences are semantically and medically relevant to the specific case.
- Establishes a new state-of-the-art on the **MIMIC-CXR** dataset by bridging the gap between raw visual data and structured clinical reporting.
key_results: Outperforms existing retrieval-based radiology models on standard NLP metrics (BLEU, ROUGE).
Demonstrates superior clinical accuracy as measured by the CheXpert labeler.
Validates the importance of region-level anatomical grounding over global image-to-text retrieval.
Proves that temporal modeling of prior studies significantly improves report fidelity for chronic conditions.
Reduces hallucinations by grounding retrieval in both visual regions and clinical metadata.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce STAR3, a framework using spatio-temporal conditioning and anatomical grounding to significantly improve multimodal radiology report retrieval.

title: Hidden Forgetting in Continual Multimodal Learning: When Accuracy Survives but Grounding Fails
summary: Multimodal large language models must continually adapt to evolving tasks and domains, yet standard continual learning metrics mainly measure whether old answers remain correct, leaving the stability of multimodal grounding largely unexamined. We study this overlooked failure mode and ask whether a continually adapted MLLM can preserve not only what it answers, but also how it uses visual, textual, OCR, chart, and document evidence. We identify \emph{hidden evidence-use forgetting}, where answer accuracy is retained while the model silently shifts toward different or less grounded evidence channels, and propose \textsc{RCL}, a replay-free reliance-constrained continual learning framework. \textsc{RCL} freezes the previous checkpoint as a behavioral reference, estimates teacher and student evidence-reliance profiles through counterfactual channel interventions, and jointly optimizes task learning, prediction preservation, and reliance preservation without adding inference-time cost. Across CoIN, COAST, MCITlib, and an evidence-sensitive multimodal stream, \textsc{RCL} consistently improves final performance and reduces forgetting over replay-free, PEFT, routing, and memory-assisted baselines, while substantially lowering modality reliance drift, dominant evidence flips, and hidden forgetting rates. These results suggest that robust continual multimodal learning requires preserving the evidence path behind correct answers, not merely the answers themselves.

processed.summary:
- Identifies **hidden evidence-use forgetting**, a phenomenon where MLLMs maintain prediction accuracy while silently shifting their underlying reasoning away from intended visual or textual evidence.
- Proposes **RCL (Reliance-Constrained Continual Learning)**, a replay-free framework that preserves the **evidence-reliance profile** of models during sequential task adaptation.
- Utilizes **counterfactual channel interventions** and behavioral reference checkpoints to align student model reliance with teacher models without requiring a rehearsal buffer.
- Reduces **modality reliance drift** and evidence flips, ensuring that the model's grounding remains stable across diverse multimodal streams like OCR, charts, and documents.
key_results: RCL consistently outperforms PEFT, routing, and memory-assisted baselines in continual multimodal benchmarks.
Discovery that standard accuracy metrics mask significant internal grounding failures in adapted MLLMs.
Significant reduction in 'hidden forgetting' rates across CoIN, COAST, and MCITlib datasets.
Successful preservation of the evidence path behind correct answers without increasing inference-time latency.
Demonstrated stability in evidence-reliance even when the model is exposed to shifting data distributions.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Nanyang Technological University researchers introduce RCL to prevent MLLMs from losing visual grounding during continual task adaptation.

title: InduceKV: Fixed-Footprint Continual Adaptation of Multimodal LLMs via Inducing KV Memories
summary: Multimodal large language models must adapt to evolving tasks and domains, yet continual improvement under bounded deployment footprint remains difficult because repeated parameter updates or growing replay stores can accumulate adaptation state over time. We study fixed-footprint continual adaptation: the deployed adaptation state is kept under a fixed memory budget, while the backbone model is left unchanged and task-specific updates are externalized. We propose InduceKV, a retrieval-based method that stores each selected training prefix as an attention-ready memory entry, consisting of a frozen retrieval key and compact layerwise key--value (KV) payloads that can be appended to the model's self-attention cache. Under a strict memory budget, InduceKV constructs a compact inducing set through bilevel selection: a lightweight calibration is fit for retrieval, while the selected memory balances current-task likelihood, anchor-based retention, and coverage in the frozen retrieval space. Across task-incremental instruction tuning, continual VQA, domain-incremental adaptation, and lifelong multimodal instruction tuning, InduceKV consistently improves over PEFT, MoE, replay, and prompt-retrieval baselines under matched memory budgets. We further report backbone-matched, stage-1 CoIN, compute-matched, and scalability diagnostics, showing that the gains are not due to a stronger backbone, replay alone, or an unbounded candidate pool.

processed.summary:
• Introduces **InduceKV**, a framework for **fixed-footprint continual adaptation** that stores training prefixes as retrieval-ready attention memories.
• Utilizes **compact layerwise KV payloads** appended to the self-attention cache, allowing task-specific updates without altering the frozen backbone.
• Implements **bilevel selection** to optimize the memory set based on likelihood, retention, and feature coverage under strict budget constraints.
• Provides a modular alternative to growing parameter sets, effectively externalizing adaptation states for **multimodal LLMs**.
key_results: Outperforms standard PEFT, MoE, and replay-based methods under matched memory budgets.
Successfully maintains performance across task-incremental, domain-incremental, and lifelong multimodal instruction tuning.
Bilevel selection mechanism balances current-task accuracy with anchor-based retention to prevent forgetting.
Maintains efficient inference by using frozen retrieval keys and a compact inducing set.
Ablation studies confirm gains are not due to stronger backbones or unbounded candidate pools.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce InduceKV, a retrieval-based system enabling fixed-footprint continual adaptation of multimodal models via compact KV memories.

title: EduArt: An educational-level benchmark for evaluating art history knowledge in large language models
summary: Large language models now score near ceiling on general benchmarks, but these aggregate measures reveal little about how models behave within single disciplines. Existing art-focused evaluations rely on synthetic questions and rarely report item-level properties. This paper introduces EduArt, an educational-level benchmark for art-historical knowledge and visual reasoning in multimodal LLMs. EduArt comprises 871 human-authored questions from Italian secondary-school exercises and US Advanced Placement Art History exams, spanning two languages and seven formats from multiple choice to in-text word placement and error identification. Twelve models from six provider families were evaluated under a default answer-only condition and a motivation condition requiring written justification, and characterized using Classical Test Theory and a logistic regression isolating the effects of format, language, image presence, and model. The benchmark showed strong psychometric properties (mean discrimination 0.514, 82.3 percent good discriminators), while multiple-choice accuracy saturated near ceiling for six models, showing recognition formats alone cannot distinguish frontier models. Format was a strong independent predictor of accuracy: models exceeding 94 percent on multiple choice fell to 23.9 percent on open completion (Claude Opus 4.6) and 6.2 percent on error identification (Claude Sonnet 4.6). The motivation condition changed accuracy in a predominantly negative, family-dependent direction. These dissociations indicate that art-historical knowledge and the ability to deploy it are distinct capabilities, and that single-format benchmarks overestimate what models can reliably do. Mapping this capability profile is a precondition for responsible use of multimodal LLMs in art-historical scholarship, where tasks demand producing and manipulating content rather than selecting from fixed options.

processed.summary:
- **EduArt** is a specialized benchmark comprising 871 human-authored questions from Italian secondary-school and US Advanced Placement Art History exams to test **multimodal reasoning**.
- The evaluation framework employs **Classical Test Theory (CTT)** and logistic regression to isolate effects of seven response formats, varying from multiple-choice to complex **error identification**.
- The study reveals a significant **performance dissociation**, where models achieving near-ceiling results in recognition tasks fail drastically in generative or manipulative contexts.
- It highlights a critical need for **domain-specific evaluation** methodologies that move beyond synthetic datasets to assess real-world expert-level scholarship capabilities.
key_results: Models exceeding 94% accuracy in multiple-choice formats plummeted to 23.9% in open completion tasks.
Claude 3 Opus and Sonnet showed extreme sensitivity to format, with error identification accuracy as low as 6.2%.
The 'motivation condition' (prompting for justification) predominantly decreased model accuracy across different families.
The benchmark demonstrated high psychometric quality with a mean discrimination index of 0.514 across 82.3% of items.
Multiple-choice accuracy is now saturated, making it an ineffective metric for distinguishing between frontier MLLMs.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce EduArt, revealing that frontier MLLMs excel at recognition but fail at generative art-historical reasoning tasks.

title: Partially-Blind Single-Qubit Classification over a Prototype Hybrid Quantum Network
summary: In the NISQ era, there is a need for resource-efficient proof-of-principle experiments that can be built up to genuine utility. Single-qubit classifiers (SQCs) are small-scale hybrid quantum-classical machines capable of performing a basic machine learning task: classifying data. In principle, these can be scaled up to many-qubit quantum classifiers capable of quantum computational advantage. Another type of quantum advantage is enabled by blind quantum computation (BQC), wherein a client may run delegated quantum computations on an untrusted server with information-theoretic security. In this paper, we develop a framework and propose a prototype experiment for a SQC where it is known to the server that a classification is being performed, but the data and outcome stay hidden, i.e., it performs partially-blind SQC (PB-SQC). This can be integrated into a quantum network to deliver quantum-secured classifications to remote clients; we study this for a heterogeneous quantum network link in which entanglement is shared between a server and a client equipped with a multiplexed solid-state quantum memory using entanglement swapping. The framework we develop for PB-SQC on this setup is tested in a simulation with realistic hardware parameters on a real-world credit card transaction fraud database with classification outcomes approaching those of its equivalent classical deep-belief network. In addition, we show how a two-qubit classifier (TQC) instead of a SQC enables verification of the computation. These results pave the way towards a short- to mid-term quantum network offering use-case-ready quantum applications.

processed.summary:
* Proposes a **Partially-Blind Single-Qubit Classification (PB-SQC)** framework designed for privacy-preserving machine learning tasks over quantum networks.
* Implements a **hybrid quantum-classical architecture** utilizing entanglement swapping and multiplexed solid-state quantum memory to hide data and outcomes from untrusted servers.
* Bridges the gap between **Blind Quantum Computation (BQC)** and practical utility by enabling delegated classification tasks on near-term (NISQ) hardware.
* Validates the framework via simulation on **real-world financial datasets**, achieving performance metrics comparable to classical deep-belief networks.
key_results: Introduction of the PB-SQC framework for secure, remote classification.
Hardware-realistic simulation using multiplexed solid-state quantum memory parameters.
Classification accuracy on credit card fraud data matching classical deep-belief network baselines.
Demonstration of entanglement swapping as a mechanism for quantum-secured data links.
Theoretical extension to two-qubit classifiers (TQC) to enable computational verification.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Quantum network researchers develop a partially-blind classification framework that secures remote data processing with near-classical performance levels.

title: Traceable Fault Diagnosis for Battery Energy Storage Systems via Retrieval-Augmented Multi-Agent O&M Assistant
summary: Large-scale battery energy storage systems (BESSs) require O&M decisions that combine alarms, cell-level measurements, device topology, diagnostic tables, historical cases, and maintenance documents. Monitoring platforms can flag threshold violations, but they often cannot explain whether voltage inconsistency, resistance drift, short-circuit risk, capacity divergence, or thermal abnormality needs intervention. This digest presents a traceable BESS fault-diagnosis assistant that uses retrieval-augmented multi-agent reasoning to connect operational data, domain knowledge, visual evidence, and report generation. Reliability is improved through BESS-specific task routing, schema-constrained natural-language database access, hybrid text-image retrieval, and evidence-based answer synthesis. Preliminary internal evaluation is reported for routing, database access, and diagnostic reasoning.

• Proposes a **multi-agent O&M assistant** that integrates real-time battery sensor data with domain knowledge through **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)**.
• Implements a **task-routing architecture** specifically designed for energy storage, utilizing **schema-constrained NL2SQL** for structured data access.
• Features **hybrid text-image retrieval** to combine maintenance documentation with visual evidence for holistic fault analysis.
• Enhances explainability in BESS monitoring by providing **traceable reasoning** for complex issues like voltage inconsistency and thermal abnormalities.
key_results: Developed a specialized multi-agent framework for Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS).
Integrated schema-constrained natural language database access for precise data querying.
Combined textual maintenance records with visual evidence for multimodal fault diagnosis.
Enabled automated, evidence-based diagnostic report generation.
Validated system performance through preliminary evaluations of task routing and database accuracy.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a multi-agent RAG framework to automate complex, traceable fault diagnosis for battery energy storage systems.

title: Episodic-to-Semantic Consolidation Without Identity Drift
summary: Long-running adaptive intelligent agents face a structural tension between knowledge consolidation and information integrity. Memory consolidation is conventionally treated as an agent-changing operation: a model is fine-tuned, a prompt rewritten, a policy distilled, or a reflection appended to the context that governs future behaviour. In regulated autonomic deployment this is a liability because the agent operates under commitments and audit contracts that bind to a specific, cryptographically certified identity. We propose to treat consolidation not as a mutation of the planner or the identity manifest, but as a deterministic function f: M^ep -> M^sem over episodic memory whose output is a separately addressable semantic knowledge layer; the identity hash does not read M^sem, so consolidation updates knowledge without changing the agent's certified identity. We give a formal account of the agent representation, prove identity invariance through a structural lemma on the manifest's hash-input set, specify a deterministic aggregation algorithm whose outputs are auditable database rows with explicit confidence and supporting-event provenance, and validate the construction with synthetic experiments demonstrating per-field correctness, byte-equal identity across consolidation passes, and a mean 79.82% reduction in unproductive planner attempts (95% BCa CI [78.02%, 81.49%] across 10 seeds) against a calibrated Bayesian-shrunk baseline. The construction is a knowledge-update discipline for autonomic agents in which lessons accumulate as queryable facts while the agent's certified identity remains byte-equal across its operational lifetime, with an embodied service agent as the running case study.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **deterministic consolidation function** that transforms episodic memory into a separate semantic knowledge layer without mutating the core agent planner.
- Solves the problem of **identity drift** by ensuring the agent's cryptographic identity hash remains unchanged while its knowledge base evolves.
- Implements an **auditable database structure** where consolidated facts include explicit confidence scores and supporting-event provenance for verification.
- Reduces **unproductive planner attempts** significantly by providing agents with queryable facts instead of requiring re-processing of raw history.
key_results: Formal proof of identity invariance through a structural lemma on hash-input sets.
Development of a deterministic aggregation algorithm for auditable semantic memory.
Achieved a mean 79.82% reduction in unproductive planner attempts.
Maintained byte-equal identity across multiple consolidation passes in synthetic trials.
Proven per-field correctness for semantic knowledge updates with explicit provenance.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose a deterministic memory consolidation method that updates agent knowledge while maintaining a cryptographically certified, byte-equal identity.

title: MolSight: A Graph-Aware Vision-Language Model for Unified Chemical Image Understanding
summary: Using molecular large language models (LLMs) as a unified framework for understanding molecular structures and functions is emerging as a new trend in tasks such as molecular design and drug discovery. However, these models struggle to fully capture the visual representation of molecular structures, limiting their potential. While existing molecular vision-language models (VLMs) show promise, they still face challenges in structural alignment and lack the necessary topological modeling for accurate molecular understanding. To address this, we propose MolSight, a graph-aware vision-language model framework designed to enhance the understanding of molecular images by VLMs. MolSight integrates a Molecular Topology Module to inject chemical-bond adjacency information into vision tokens, and a Molecular Grounding Module to align visual features with chemical symbolic semantics. Our experiments demonstrate that MolSight significantly outperforms existing VLMs, molecular LLMs, and specialized tools across multiple chemical visual understanding tasks, achieving a new level of molecular image reasoning.

* Proposes **MolSight**, a specialized Vision-Language Model (VLM) designed to bridge the gap between visual molecular depictions and **topological understanding**.
* Introduces a **Molecular Topology Module** that injects chemical-bond adjacency data into vision tokens, effectively teaching the model to "read" the 2D structure graph.
* Utilizes a **Molecular Grounding Module** to align latent visual features with symbolic chemical semantics, ensuring precise mapping to **molecular identifiers**.
* Establishes a unified framework for complex **chemical image reasoning**, outperforming general-purpose VLMs and specialized domain-specific tools.
key_results: MolSight outperforms state-of-the-art VLMs in molecular image-to-text translation tasks.
Integrated topology modules significantly improve model accuracy in identifying chemical bond structures.
Superior alignment achieved between visual representations and symbolic chemical semantics.
Demonstrates enhanced performance in zero-shot chemical visual reasoning benchmarks.
The framework provides a scalable approach for drug discovery and molecular design using vision-first architectures.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Tencent AI Lab introduces MolSight, a graph-aware VLM that enhances molecular image understanding through topological and grounding modules.

title: Epic-Organized vs. Requirement-Aligned Gherkin: An Empirical Evaluation of LLM-Based Acceptance Criteria Generation
summary: Automated authoring of Gherkin Behavior-Driven Development (BDD) acceptance criteria remains a manual bottleneck in requirements engineering. This study investigates whether epic-organized LLM-generated Gherkin produces higher quality and coverage than requirement-aligned generation. We compare our Timeless (an epic-organized LLM pipeline) approach against a naive large language model (LLM) baseline on four requirements documents (107 requirements) from the PURE dataset. Evaluation covers structural metrics, automated requirement coverage via TF-IDF and dense embeddings, and blind expert assessment by four researchers. In our evaluation, the JSON-constrained pipeline produced structurally valid scenarios across all generated outputs, while the zero-shot baseline achieved 99% structural validity. Semantic coverage was comparable to the baseline, with Timeless achieving 94.3% semantic Requirement Coverage Rate compared with 92.9% for the baseline. TF-IDF produced lower coverage scores for the epic-organized output, suggesting that lexical metrics may miss coverage when scenarios paraphrase requirements at a higher level of abstraction. Expert raters prefer the epic-organized strategy on Correctness (4.61 vs 4.14), Executability (4.61 vs 4.07), and Completeness (4.31 vs 3.50). Overall, the results suggest that epic-organized generation can improve perceived Gherkin quality while maintaining comparable semantic coverage, although broader replication is needed before generalizing this finding.

* Introduces **Timeless**, a specialized LLM pipeline that utilizes an **epic-organized strategy** to automate the generation of Gherkin-style Behavior-Driven Development (BDD) acceptance criteria.
* Implements a **JSON-constrained architecture** to enforce structural validity, achieving 100% compliance in generated scenarios compared to 99% for zero-shot baselines.
* Utilizes a dual-evaluation framework combining **automated metrics** (TF-IDF and dense embeddings) with blind expert assessment to measure both lexical and semantic requirement coverage.
* Highlights that high-level **hierarchical organization** in prompts significantly improves human-perceived executability and completeness over requirement-aligned generation.

Semantic Requirement Coverage Rate reached 94.3%, outperforming the baseline's 92.9%.
Expert raters scored the epic-organized approach significantly higher in Completeness (4.31 vs 3.50).
Correctness and Executability metrics both peaked at 4.61/5.0 using the structured pipeline.
Lexical metrics like TF-IDF were found to be less effective than dense embeddings for evaluating paraphrased requirements.
